families.classification: public layer-one ecosystem; current implementation details are only partially documented in the reviewed public sources
families.nativeArchitecture: Open Network mainnet with a Pi Browser application platform, client SDK, server Platform API, user authentication, and user-to-app or app-to-user payment flows.
families.consensusModel: The original whitepaper describes a Stellar Consensus Protocol / Federated Byzantine Agreement lineage. Current public implementation-level parameters and node-selection details need verification.
families.transactionModel: The current developer surface exposes authenticated Pi users and payment lifecycles; transaction internals below the Platform API are not inferred here.
families.executionModel: Reviewed current sources document Pi apps and Platform API workflows, not a general permissionless smart-contract VM; do not imply one.
families.finalityModel: Needs current implementation-level official verification; gameplay must not invent timing or threshold guarantees.
families.feeModel: Not specified in the reviewed current developer sources at sufficient protocol detail; no live amount or price claim is permitted.
families.interoperabilityModel: Open Network enables external connectivity; the SDK and Platform API connect Pi Browser apps to authenticated platform services.
families.privacyModel: Authentication returns platform-scoped user information under API rules; application backends must minimize data and never expose server credentials in clients.
gameplayOpportunities: simulate safe app authentication and server verification
explore quorum and trust concepts as historically documented
distinguish Testnet, Mainnet, and Open Network
unsuitableSimplifications: daily tapping validates blocks
a mining-session button performs proof of work
security circles alone guarantee consensus
Pi SDK payments expose private keys
historical whitepaper text proves current implementation details
Rush Pi is officially affiliated with Pi Network
families.simplificationNotes: Only current developer workflows and Open Network status are treated as current. Consensus and social-trust modules remain historical or explicitly unresolved.
unresolvedQuestions: Which current public protocol specification defines Pi consensus messages, quorum configuration, and finality?
What current implementation role do security circles play?
Is a separate trademark license required for the product name Rush Pi?

statusLayers.claim: Open Network launched in February 2025 and supports external connectivity.
Pi Browser apps can initialize the SDK, authenticate users, and create documented payment flows with server-side completion and verification.
The original architecture describes SCP/FBA concepts and social trust or security circles.
Quorum-graph puzzles are educational models, not claims about live Pi validator configuration.
Daily app participation is not represented as block validation, proof of work, or a direct consensus vote.
Current node-selection, finality thresholds, and the operational role of social-trust signals require more current official technical documentation.

families.nativeArchitecture: A peer-to-peer chain of proof-of-work blocks whose nodes validate transactions and maintain the unspent transaction output set.
families.consensusModel: Proof of work with a best-chain rule based on accumulated work.
families.transactionModel: UTXO inputs consume prior unspent outputs and create new outputs protected by spending conditions.
families.executionModel: Transaction scripts validate spend conditions; this profile does not model Bitcoin as a general account-based VM.
families.finalityModel: Probabilistic confidence grows as more valid work builds on the containing block; reorganizations remain possible.
families.feeModel: Transactions can offer fees derived from the difference between input and output value; inclusion policy and demand affect selection.
families.interoperabilityModel: Base protocol does not provide a universal cross-chain messaging layer; external protocols must state separate trust assumptions.
families.privacyModel: Pseudonymous public transaction graph; addresses and UTXOs do not by themselves provide transaction confidentiality.
unsuitableSimplifications: one confirmation is absolutely final
wallets contain coins as files
mining means solving a knowledge quiz
all outputs belong to accounts
families.simplificationNotes: Gameplay uses small discrete outputs and a visible difficulty gate, not real mining economics or live fees.

families.nativeArchitecture: Account-based state machine with externally owned accounts, contract accounts, an execution environment, and proof-of-stake consensus.
families.consensusModel: Proof of stake with block proposals, attestations, fork choice, and checkpoint finality.
families.transactionModel: Signed account transactions include a nonce, destination or contract creation, value, data, gas limit, and fee fields.
families.executionModel: EVM smart-contract execution is deterministic and metered by gas; transactions can complete or revert.
families.finalityModel: Protocol checkpoint finality plus earlier head selection; applications distinguish inclusion from finalization.
families.feeModel: EIP-1559-style base fee plus optional priority fee within transaction fee caps; all game units remain abstract.
families.interoperabilityModel: General smart contracts and rollup settlement support many external protocols, each with independent bridge or messaging assumptions.
families.privacyModel: Public account and contract state by default; privacy requires additional application or cryptographic systems.
unsuitableSimplifications: gas is fuel stored inside a contract
every included transaction is immediately finalized
a reverted call consumes no resources
all layer-two systems have identical security
families.simplificationNotes: Puzzles model nonce, gas, and reversion with fixed teaching units and omit current network amounts.

families.nativeArchitecture: Programs operate on explicitly supplied accounts, separating executable code from data accounts and exposing account access for scheduling.
families.consensusModel: A proof-of-stake design using Proof of History as a cryptographic clock component and Tower-style BFT concepts; live parameters are outside this profile.
families.transactionModel: Transactions contain instructions referencing programs and the accounts those instructions read or write.
families.executionModel: Transactions with non-conflicting account access can be scheduled in parallel; conflicting writable access is serialized.
families.finalityModel: Commitment levels and consensus progress must be distinguished; the game makes no fixed timing guarantee.
families.feeModel: Protocol transaction and compute-resource fees exist; no current amounts are embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: External bridges and messaging systems are separate integrations with their own assumptions.
families.privacyModel: Account and transaction data are public by default.
unsuitableSimplifications: Proof of History alone is consensus
all transactions always run in parallel
accounts are user balances only
performance claims without current measurement context
families.simplificationNotes: The scheduling puzzle focuses on account conflicts and avoids throughput numbers or volatile runtime parameters.

families.classification: public payment-oriented ledger using federated agreement
families.nativeArchitecture: Account-based ledger with operations grouped into transactions and protocol-native payment, trustline, and exchange functions.
families.consensusModel: Stellar Consensus Protocol, a Federated Byzantine Agreement design using quorum sets and slices.
families.transactionModel: An account transaction contains one or more ordered operations and uses sequence numbers and signatures.
families.executionModel: Protocol-defined operations execute atomically as a transaction rather than through a general EVM.
families.finalityModel: SCP externalizes ledger values under quorum-intersection and liveness assumptions.
families.feeModel: Transactions specify resource-related fees under network rules; no current amounts are embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: Assets, trustlines, anchors, and path payments support routing within Stellar; external-chain messaging is separate.
families.privacyModel: Ledger activity is public by default; trustlines and accounts are not confidential identity proofs.
unsuitableSimplifications: trust edges mean friendship
any overlapping graph is safe
path payments guarantee best external-market price
Stellar and Pi current implementations are identical
families.simplificationNotes: Graph puzzles teach quorum intersection conceptually and path puzzles use fictional assets.

families.classification: public multi-chain platform with repeated-sampling consensus family
families.nativeArchitecture: Network of validators supporting distinct virtual machines and chain structures; the foundational protocol family includes Snow and Avalanche variants.
families.consensusModel: Repeated random subsampling drives metastable preference and confidence; deployed chains may use protocol-family variants.
families.transactionModel: Depends on the specific Avalanche chain or VM; the profile does not collapse all subnets and chains into one state model.
families.executionModel: VM-specific execution, including EVM-compatible and application-specific environments.
families.finalityModel: Protocol-family acceptance follows confidence thresholds; exact deployed parameters and timing are intentionally omitted.
families.feeModel: Chain and VM specific; no live fee schedule is represented.
families.interoperabilityModel: Multiple Avalanche chains and network constructs can interoperate through defined platform mechanisms and external bridges.
families.privacyModel: Public by default unless a particular VM or application adds privacy.
unsuitableSimplifications: DAG shape alone produces consensus
one sample finalizes a choice
every Avalanche chain uses an identical VM
sampling peers is a social popularity vote
families.simplificationNotes: A small deterministic sampling sequence illustrates feedback but not deployed randomness or parameters.

families.nativeArchitecture: A relay-chain security and consensus system validates parachain state-transition candidates produced by collators.
families.consensusModel: Relay-chain proof-of-stake consensus plus parachain validation and availability processes.
families.transactionModel: Parachain-specific; each parachain defines its state machine while submitting candidates to shared validation.
families.executionModel: Parachain runtimes execute application-specific logic; the relay system verifies commitments and validity under the protocol.
families.finalityModel: Relay-chain finality secures accepted relay and parachain commitments under shared-security rules.
families.feeModel: Parachain-specific transaction fees and protocol resource mechanisms; no universal live amount is assumed.
families.interoperabilityModel: XCM expresses cross-consensus instructions and messaging among compatible consensus systems.
families.privacyModel: Public by default; parachains may define additional privacy behavior.
unsuitableSimplifications: parachains borrow coins as security
XCM is a bridge contract
every parachain has the same execution model
shared security removes all application risk
families.simplificationNotes: The relay-core metaphor is bounded to validation and finality concepts, not total application safety.

families.classification: ecosystem and stack for sovereign application-specific blockchains
families.nativeArchitecture: Modular chains commonly combine Cosmos SDK application logic, CometBFT consensus, and IBC interoperability.
families.consensusModel: Typically BFT consensus through CometBFT in this profile; Cosmos is an ecosystem, not one global validator set.
families.transactionModel: Application-defined messages processed by SDK modules and chain-specific ante or execution rules.
families.executionModel: Deterministic application state machines assembled from modules or custom logic.
families.finalityModel: BFT-style finality on each sovereign chain under its validator and network assumptions.
families.feeModel: Chain-specific transaction fees and denominations; no universal current amount exists.
families.interoperabilityModel: IBC clients, connections, channels, packets, acknowledgements, and timeouts connect compatible chains.
families.privacyModel: Chain-specific; IBC packets do not automatically make application data private.
unsuitableSimplifications: Cosmos is one chain
relayers are trusted to validate packets
IBC works without client verification
all zones share security automatically
families.simplificationNotes: The module board and packet rail teach architecture while preserving chain sovereignty and light-client assumptions.

families.nativeArchitecture: Extended UTXO ledger with multi-asset outputs, scripts, data, and Ouroboros-family proof-of-stake consensus.
families.consensusModel: Ouroboros-family proof of stake; current era parameters are outside this stable educational profile.
families.transactionModel: eUTXO transactions consume explicit outputs and create new outputs while validator scripts inspect controlled transaction context.
families.executionModel: Scripts validate transactions against datum, redeemer, and context; the transaction succeeds or fails as a whole.
families.finalityModel: Chain-selection confidence under Ouroboros-family assumptions; the game avoids a fixed confirmation promise.
families.feeModel: Protocol-defined transaction fees based on transaction characteristics; no current coefficients are embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: External bridges and sidechains are separate systems; no universal base-layer messaging claim is made.
families.privacyModel: Ledger activity is public by default.
unsuitableSimplifications: eUTXO is an account model with boxes
validator scripts can inspect arbitrary changing global state
all independent outputs can always execute together
paper-era parameters are current guarantees
families.simplificationNotes: Uses a named eUTXO example and local validation puzzle, omitting era-specific ledger rules.

families.nativeArchitecture: Bitcoin-derived transparent ledger plus protocol-defined shielded pools using commitments, nullifiers, and zero-knowledge proofs.
families.consensusModel: Proof-of-work chain in the reviewed protocol lineage; current network upgrade parameters are not reproduced here.
families.transactionModel: Transactions may involve transparent and shielded value pools under consensus rules.
families.executionModel: Protocol validation verifies signatures, commitments, nullifiers, and proofs rather than a general EVM.
families.finalityModel: Probabilistic chain confirmation; privacy proof validity does not itself provide consensus finality.
families.feeModel: Protocol and wallet policies apply; no current amount is included.
families.interoperabilityModel: Cross-system bridges are separate and can change privacy or trust guarantees.
families.privacyModel: Shielded protocols conceal selected transfer details; privacy depends on correct pool use, wallet behavior, and metadata hygiene.
unsuitableSimplifications: all Zcash activity is shielded
zero knowledge means no metadata can ever leak
privacy proof finalizes a block
shielded means untraceable under every usage pattern
families.simplificationNotes: Uses abstract notes and nullifiers without reproducing cryptography or promising anonymity.

families.nativeArchitecture: UTXO-like one-time outputs combined with ring-signature and confidential-transaction techniques in its protocol lineage.
families.consensusModel: Proof-of-work chain; current algorithm parameters are intentionally omitted from this stable profile.
families.transactionModel: Transactions consume one-time outputs while privacy mechanisms obscure links and amounts according to protocol rules.
families.executionModel: Protocol transaction validation rather than a general-purpose smart-contract VM.
families.finalityModel: Probabilistic chain confirmation; privacy mechanisms are separate from fork choice.
families.feeModel: Protocol and wallet fee rules apply; no current amount is included.
families.interoperabilityModel: External exchanges, bridges, or wrapped representations add separate trust and privacy risks.
families.privacyModel: Privacy-focused defaults combine multiple techniques, but network metadata and implementation behavior remain relevant.
unsuitableSimplifications: ring members are co-signers
privacy is mathematically absolute in every context
RingCT is consensus
one privacy design can represent Zcash and Monero interchangeably
families.simplificationNotes: The profile stays conceptual because the reviewed primary paper is historical relative to evolving protocol details.
unresolvedQuestions: Which current official Monero specification should anchor active transaction-format details for a future implementation?

families.nativeArchitecture: Account-based ledger with cryptographic sortition used to select ephemeral committee participants.
families.consensusModel: Pure Proof of Stake with verifiable random selection and Byzantine agreement committees.
families.transactionModel: Account transactions and atomic groups under protocol-defined validation.
families.executionModel: Protocol transactions and application logic execute deterministically under resource constraints.
families.finalityModel: Blocks reach deterministic finality under the documented consensus assumptions, without routine forks in the protocol model.
families.feeModel: Protocol-defined fees and resource limits; current values are not embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: External bridging and state-proof integrations must preserve their specific assumptions.
families.privacyModel: Public ledger by default.
unsuitableSimplifications: every holder manually votes on every block
sortition reveals committees far in advance
finality removes all application risk
stake size alone deterministically selects one winner
families.simplificationNotes: Committee cards explain private selection and thresholds without current network parameter values.

families.nativeArchitecture: Account-based application platform using Nightshade sharding concepts and human-readable account identifiers.
families.consensusModel: Proof of stake coordinates block and chunk production across the sharded architecture.
families.transactionModel: Signed transactions initiate actions and asynchronous receipts across accounts and contracts.
families.executionModel: Contracts and account actions execute in shard contexts; cross-contract calls can proceed asynchronously.
families.finalityModel: Consensus finality applies under protocol assumptions; exact timing and live shard layout are omitted.
families.feeModel: Gas and storage-related resource accounting under protocol rules; no current values are embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: Cross-contract and cross-shard receipts are native; cross-chain systems are separate integrations.
families.privacyModel: Public account and contract state by default; access keys support scoped control, not confidentiality.
unsuitableSimplifications: each shard is an independent chain with no coordination
human-readable accounts reveal verified human identity
cross-shard actions are one synchronous call
all keys have full account control
families.simplificationNotes: Room routing illustrates shards and receipts without volatile live shard counts or performance claims.

families.nativeArchitecture: Move-based platform whose state is organized into uniquely identified owned or shared objects with versions and explicit transaction inputs.
families.consensusModel: Proof-of-stake validator system with transaction paths influenced by object ownership and shared-state coordination.
families.transactionModel: Transactions consume or access object inputs and produce created, mutated, transferred, wrapped, or deleted objects.
families.executionModel: Independent owned-object transactions expose parallelism; shared-object transactions require coordinated ordering.
families.finalityModel: Protocol-certified outcomes under validator assumptions; the game avoids current latency claims.
families.feeModel: Gas and storage economics are protocol-defined; no current amounts are embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: External bridges are distinct systems; object ownership semantics do not automatically transfer across chains.
families.privacyModel: Objects and transactions are public by default unless an application adds privacy techniques.
unsuitableSimplifications: objects are NFTs only
every object transaction skips consensus
ownership removes the need for signatures
parallelism is unlimited
families.simplificationNotes: Object capsules communicate identity, version, ownership, and dependency while omitting the full Move type system.

families.nativeArchitecture: Account-based Move platform with a pipelined architecture and optimistic parallel execution through Block-STM.
families.consensusModel: AptosBFT-family proof-of-stake consensus orders transactions or blocks before deterministic execution.
families.transactionModel: Account transactions call Move entry functions and carry sequence, gas, and authentication data.
families.executionModel: Block-STM speculatively executes transactions in parallel, detects conflicts, and re-executes as required for a deterministic result.
families.finalityModel: BFT commit finality under validator and network assumptions; exact live timing is not included.
families.feeModel: Gas-metered transactions under network rules; no current schedule is embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: External bridges and messaging systems are separate trust domains.
families.privacyModel: Account and transaction state are public by default.
unsuitableSimplifications: parallel execution changes the deterministic result
users must predeclare all access for Block-STM
aborted speculation means the transaction permanently failed
Move makes contracts automatically safe
families.simplificationNotes: A scheduling factory teaches conflicts and retries without benchmark or throughput claims.

families.classification: public account-based payment ledger with trust-based BFT consensus
families.nativeArchitecture: Servers maintain complete ledger state and process deterministic account transactions into successive validated ledger versions.
families.consensusModel: Validators iteratively propose transaction sets, evaluated through each server's trusted-validator list, then publish validations for a resulting ledger.
families.transactionModel: Signed account transactions change ledger objects and can have provisional outcomes before appearing in a validated ledger.
families.executionModel: Protocol-defined transaction types execute in canonical order; successful and certain failed transactions can be included with final result codes.
families.finalityModel: Applications rely on validated ledgers and final result conditions, not provisional submission responses.
families.feeModel: Transaction cost and queue rules deter spam and adapt under load; no current amounts are embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: Payment paths and issued assets are native ledger features; sidechains and bridges have separate rules.
families.privacyModel: Ledger state and transaction history are public; trusted-validator selection is not user identity privacy.
unsuitableSimplifications: a provisional success is final
trusted validators can arbitrarily rewrite transaction rules
UNLs are one mandatory identical list
XRPL consensus is proof of stake
families.simplificationNotes: The briefing emphasizes candidate versus validated state and avoids live settlement-time claims.

families.nativeArchitecture: Hashgraph event-gossip data structure and consensus services rather than a native linear chain of transaction blocks; EVM-compatible block views are an interface layer.
families.consensusModel: Hashgraph consensus derives ordering and timestamps from gossiped events and virtual voting concepts.
families.transactionModel: Account and service transactions receive consensus order and timestamps.
families.executionModel: Native services plus an EVM smart-contract service; the profile keeps these execution surfaces distinct.
families.finalityModel: Consensus order becomes final under Hashgraph protocol assumptions; no current latency figure is embedded.
families.feeModel: Service-specific fees exist; no live values or fiat comparisons are included.
families.interoperabilityModel: EVM tooling and external bridge services are interfaces with separate assumptions.
families.privacyModel: Public ledger and mirror-node data by default.
gameplayOpportunities: distinguish native events from EVM block views
unsuitableSimplifications: Hashgraph is a chain of blocks
DAG shape alone equals consensus
EVM block grouping drives native consensus
consensus timestamps prove off-ledger event truth
families.simplificationNotes: A gossip weave demonstrates causal event propagation while explicitly avoiding a blockchain label for native Hashgraph consensus.
unresolvedQuestions: Which current official technical paper URL should anchor a deeper virtual-voting implementation module?

families.classification: public blockchain for verifiable storage markets
families.nativeArchitecture: Storage providers make deals, seal sectors, submit storage proofs, and participate in a tipset-based blockchain.
families.consensusModel: Expected Consensus uses storage power and Winning Proof-of-Spacetime in block production, with heaviest-chain selection.
families.transactionModel: Messages invoke built-in actors or compatible contract execution while storage deals and proofs update chain state.
families.executionModel: Protocol actors and smart-contract environment process deterministic messages.
families.finalityModel: Heaviest-chain soft finality plus protocol-defined finality policy; current numeric thresholds are not embedded.
families.feeModel: Message execution and inclusion fees plus storage-deal economics; no price or earning estimate is shown.
families.interoperabilityModel: Content addressing complements IPFS; external chains and bridges remain separate systems.
families.privacyModel: Deals, proofs, and chain state are public; stored content confidentiality requires encryption and key management outside proof of storage.
unsuitableSimplifications: proof of storage means the file is public
a valid proof guarantees fast retrieval
IPFS and Filecoin are the same network
storage-provider rewards are guaranteed
families.simplificationNotes: Storage cells and proof deadlines are abstract; no capacity, collateral, reward, or price figure is used.

families.nativeArchitecture: A blockweave-style historical dataset where block production includes proof of access to selected prior data chunks.
families.consensusModel: Storage-oriented proof-of-work lineage using Succinct Proofs of Random Access and protocol-defined block validation.
families.transactionModel: Transactions can upload data, transfer protocol units, or both, with content data organized for verification and retrieval.
families.executionModel: Base-protocol transactions plus separate application and compute layers; those layers are not conflated with base consensus.
families.finalityModel: Heaviest-valid-history selection under protocol assumptions; permanent-storage language is not translated into absolute physical certainty.
families.feeModel: Upfront storage-endowment design and protocol fees; no live storage cost or return is included.
families.interoperabilityModel: Gateways, bundlers, and compute layers extend access but have roles distinct from base consensus.
families.privacyModel: Uploaded data is intended for durable public availability unless encrypted before upload; encryption-key loss remains outside protocol recovery.
unsuitableSimplifications: permanent means physically impossible to lose
a gateway is the blockchain
proof of access proves all data is held by every miner
uploading confidential data makes it private
families.simplificationNotes: Recall-chunk play teaches proof of access without cost, profitability, or absolute permanence promises.

families.classification: decentralized oracle and cross-chain service family, not one general-purpose blockchain
families.nativeArchitecture: Service-specific decentralized oracle networks collect, aggregate, attest, or relay data and messages for consuming smart contracts.
families.consensusModel: No single universal chain consensus model; each service uses its documented oracle-network and destination-verification architecture.
families.transactionModel: Reports, requests, responses, and cross-chain messages are submitted to supported networks through service-specific contracts.
families.executionModel: Off-chain observation or computation combines with on-chain verification and consumer-contract logic.
families.finalityModel: Depends on the source and destination chains plus service-specific report acceptance; oracle agreement is not base-chain finality.
families.feeModel: Service and chain specific; no current amount or token-price claim is included.
families.interoperabilityModel: Data feeds, functions, automation, VRF, and CCIP solve distinct external-data or cross-chain tasks and must not be merged into one mechanic.
families.privacyModel: Public reports are visible on their destination chains; private data needs a separately documented service or application design.
gameplayOpportunities: route a cross-chain message with service-specific guards
unsuitableSimplifications: an oracle makes external data objectively true
Chainlink is a layer-one blockchain
all services share one security model
price feeds should use live market data in the game
families.simplificationNotes: Uses fictional weather and sensor reports; avoids live prices and keeps service labels explicit.

families.classification: layer-two scaling family deriving settlement security from a base layer
families.nativeArchitecture: Off-chain execution and state with compressed transaction data or commitments posted to base-layer contracts and a dispute process for invalid assertions.
families.consensusModel: Ordering commonly comes from a sequencer; correctness and settlement derive from base-layer data, contracts, and fault-proof rules rather than an independent universal consensus model.
families.transactionModel: Layer-two transactions are sequenced into batches and commitments posted to the base layer.
families.executionModel: A rollup VM executes transactions off chain; fault proofs can re-execute or narrow a disputed computation according to the implementation.
families.finalityModel: Soft confirmation can precede base-layer settlement; trust-minimized finality depends on challenge resolution and base-layer finality.
families.feeModel: Users share execution and base-layer data costs; no current amount or savings claim is embedded.
families.interoperabilityModel: Deposits, withdrawals, and messages use rollup bridge contracts and delayed or forced-inclusion paths specific to each design.
families.privacyModel: Transaction data needed for rollup verification is generally public; optimistic execution is not a privacy mechanism.
unsuitableSimplifications: optimistic means transactions are trusted forever
sequencer confirmation equals base-layer finality
every rollup has the same challenge implementation
layer two inherits all security without contract or data assumptions
families.simplificationNotes: A challenge-clock puzzle teaches the family-level trade-off without fixed withdrawal periods or performance claims.

families.nativeArchitecture: Off-chain batch execution produces state commitments and cryptographic validity proofs verified by base-layer contracts.
families.consensusModel: Sequencer or operator ordering is distinct from proof-verified correctness; base-layer consensus settles accepted commitments.
families.transactionModel: Layer-two transactions are batched, executed, compressed, and represented by state updates plus required data.
families.executionModel: A rollup VM and proof circuit encode valid state transitions; a prover produces proofs for a base-layer verifier.
families.finalityModel: Proof verification can validate state without a fraud-challenge window, while final settlement still follows base-layer finality and data assumptions.
families.feeModel: Users share proving, execution, and base-layer data costs; no current amount or universal advantage is claimed.
families.interoperabilityModel: Bridge contracts and proof-verified messaging are implementation specific.
families.privacyModel: Zero-knowledge technology does not mean every ZK rollup hides transaction data; validity and privacy are separate design choices.
gameplayOpportunities: generate and verify a validity proof
unsuitableSimplifications: ZK automatically means private transactions
a proof is generated for free
all ZK VMs are EVM-equivalent
proof verification eliminates data availability requirements
families.simplificationNotes: The proof prism stands for validity only; privacy is never inferred from the ZK label.
